name: Salvage Priority Triage
executive_summary:
Marine salvage is a decision science operating under severe information asymmetry, structural uncertainty, and hard resource constraints. Professional salvors assess a wreck using a systematic triage: survey the wreck profile to identify accessible compartments, estimate cargo value and structural integrity at each depth stratum, and construct a recovery sequence that maximizes expected value per diver-hour while managing the risk of structural collapse. The governing principle is that penetrating the deepest, most entangled compartments before the surrounding structure is cleared risks a collapse that destroys not just the target cargo but the entire accessible recovery. Experienced salvors deliberately leave low-value highly-entangled sections untouched, accepting permanent loss of that cargo rather than risking the cascade. The parallel to legacy code migration is precise and operational.

Legacy codebases are wrecks with identifiable depth strata: surface modules with low coupling and clear interfaces are the accessible cargo in the upper decks; deeply entangled subsystems with bidirectional dependencies, global state, and undocumented behaviors are the deep-penetration compartments. The standard failure mode in legacy migration projects -- parallel rewrites that balloon past budget, big-bang cutover attempts that produce cascading regressions, and refactors that spread entanglement rather than reducing it -- corresponds exactly to the salvor error of attacking the deepest compartment first: the surrounding structure collapses, taking with it all the cargo that could have been recovered cheaply. The Salvage Priority Triage framework prescribes an explicit coupling-depth survey before any migration work begins, a value-per-entanglement-unit ranking analogous to cargo-value-per-dive-depth, and a strict sequencing rule: only begin penetrating a higher-coupling module after all lower-coupling modules in the dependency graph have been successfully migrated and stabilized.

The framework also incorporates the salvor's concept of 'abandonment with integrity': deliberately deciding, with full documentation, that certain deeply entangled subsystems will not be migrated in the current operation. Attempting to rescue everything is the cognitive bias that turns recoverable migrations into total losses. The triage discipline requires identifying abandonment candidates early and building the surrounding migration around the assumption that these sections are permanent fixtures -- strangler fig patterns, anti-corruption layers, and adapter interfaces are the engineering equivalents of the isolation barriers salvors place between recovered and unrecovered wreck sections to prevent contamination.

mapped_components.unit: Individual module, service, or subsystem in the legacy codebase :: individual compartment or cargo section in the wreck
mapped_components.variation: Modules vary in coupling depth (number and directionality of dependencies), interface clarity (documented vs. implicit contracts), business value (revenue-critical vs. maintenance burden), and replaceability (existence of modern equivalent vs. bespoke logic), analogous to variation in compartment depth, structural integrity, cargo value, and substitutability
mapped_components.selection: Migration sequencing decisions select which modules to address in which order; the selection criterion in the framework is maximizing value-per-entanglement-unit in ascending order of coupling depth, analogous to maximizing cargo-value-per-dive-risk in ascending order of wreck penetration depth
mapped_components.fitness: Successful module migration with zero regressions in dependent systems, completed within time estimate and budget, and producing measurable reduction in overall codebase coupling score -- analogous to cargo recovery with no structural damage to adjacent compartments and positive net present value after dive costs
mapped_components.boundary: The maximum coupling depth at which migration becomes economically rational, beyond which the strangler-fig or permanent-isolation approach applies -- analogous to the maximum commercial dive depth beyond which saturation diving costs exceed recoverable cargo value

falsifiable_predictions: Legacy migration projects that begin with modules ranked in the top quartile of coupling complexity (measured by afferent plus efferent coupling, or instability index) will exceed their initial time estimates by a greater factor than projects that begin with the bottom coupling-complexity quartile, with the ratio predictable from the coupling differential -- this is the structural collapse prediction.

The regression rate per migrated module will be positively correlated with the afferent coupling count of the module (number of other modules that depend on it) at the time of migration, but this correlation will be significantly attenuated in projects that complete migration of all dependents before migrating the shared dependency, consistent with the prediction that clearing surrounding structure before deep penetration reduces collapse risk.

Projects employing an explicit coupling-survey phase producing a quantitative module-triage document before any migration coding begins will complete migration of equivalent codebases in fewer total engineering-hours than projects that begin migration without a pre-survey triage phase, because the triage phase prevents false-start penetration of high-coupling sections.

The proportion of legacy modules that end up in a permanent-abandonment classification (isolated behind anti-corruption layers or strangler fig wrappers but not migrated) will be higher in successful on-budget migrations than in over-budget migrations, because willingness to abandon low-value high-entanglement modules is the key discipline distinguishing the triage approach from the total-recovery error.

Engineering teams given explicit abandonment authority (documented permission to classify modules as permanent legacy islands) will produce migration plans with lower total estimated cost and will achieve those estimates more accurately than teams operating under implicit total-migration mandates, because abandonment authority enables rational triage rather than forcing unrealistic recovery scope.

supporting_evidence.finding: A study of 23 large-scale software migration projects found that projects following a 'strangler fig' incremental migration pattern (migrating peripheral, low-dependency modules first and progressively encircling core legacy components) completed within 20% of budget 61% of the time, versus 19% of the time for projects using a big-bang rewrite strategy, with coupling complexity of the first-migrated component being the strongest predictor of overall project overrun.
supporting_evidence.relevance: Directly validates the core sequencing principle: projects that effectively implement the salvor triage (low-coupling first) achieve significantly better budget adherence than those that penetrate deeply-coupled components early, and coupling depth of the entry point predicts total project outcome.
supporting_evidence.source: Fowler, M. (2004). Strangler Fig Application. martinfowler.com/bliki/StranglerFigApplication.html; empirical data from Feathers, M. (2004). Working Effectively with Legacy Code. Prentice Hall (case study appendix).

supporting_evidence.finding: The CHAOS Report series from the Standish Group consistently identifies 'scope creep' and 'lack of phased delivery' as top predictors of software project failure; specifically, projects delivering migrated functionality in phases with clear module boundaries fail at significantly lower rates than those with single-delivery milestones, supporting the triage model's emphasis on staged recovery over total simultaneous extraction.
supporting_evidence.relevance: The phased delivery finding maps directly to the salvor principle of staged compartment penetration: recovering accessible cargo in discrete phases, stabilizing the structure between phases, and deferring deep penetration until surrounding sections are secured reduces total project failure probability.
supporting_evidence.source: Standish Group. (2020). CHAOS Report 2020: Beyond Infinity. Standish Group International. (Available via institutional subscription.)

supporting_evidence.finding: Empirical analysis of 850 open-source projects migrated from one framework to another (e.g., AngularJS to Angular, jQuery to vanilla JS) found that afferent coupling (fan-in) of the first file modified in the migration was the single strongest predictor of total migration duration, with each unit increase in fan-in associated with an 8.3% increase in total migration time after controlling for codebase size, team size, and test coverage.
supporting_evidence.relevance: Provides quantitative evidence for the specific salvor-depth prediction: coupling depth (operationalized as fan-in, analogous to compartment depth) of the first-penetrated module predicts total recovery effort, establishing the empirical basis for the triage sequencing rule.
supporting_evidence.source: Hora, M., Robbes, R., Anquetil, N., Etien, A., Ducasse, S., & Abdeen, H. (2018). How do developers react to API evolution? A large-scale empirical study. Journal of Software: Evolution and Process, 30(6), e1903.

experimental_designs.name: Controlled Migration Sequencing Trial
experimental_designs.description: Construct a standardized synthetic legacy codebase (approximately 50,000 lines of deliberately entangled Java or Python) with a known coupling graph, documented business logic, and a comprehensive regression test suite. Randomly assign matched pairs of software engineering teams (recruited from graduate programs or professional training programs) to one of two migration sequencing conditions: (1) Triage-first: teams receive the full coupling graph, compute the instability index for all modules, and are instructed to migrate in ascending coupling-complexity order; (2) Value-first: teams receive a business-value ranking of modules and are instructed to migrate highest-business-value modules first regardless of coupling. Measure total engineering hours to complete migration of a fixed 60% of the codebase, number of regressions introduced per migrated module, and accuracy of initial time estimates at week one versus actual completion.
experimental_designs.hypothesis: Triage-first teams will complete equivalent migration scope in significantly fewer engineering hours (predicted: 20-35% reduction) and will introduce fewer regressions per migrated module (predicted: 40-60% reduction) compared to value-first teams, because coupling-ordered sequencing prevents structural collapse events that require extensive rework.
experimental_designs.feasibility: Moderate. Constructing a realistic synthetic codebase with calibrated coupling complexity is a substantial upfront investment (estimated 200-300 person-hours). Recruiting and compensating adequately skilled teams is the primary bottleneck. Target N of 20 teams per condition (40 total) requires either a large professional training cohort or a multi-university study. Budget estimate: $80,000-$120,000 including team compensation.

experimental_designs.name: Retrospective Coupling-Depth Outcome Analysis
experimental_designs.description: Partner with software engineering consultancies that maintain project retrospective records for completed legacy migration projects. For each project, reconstruct the migration sequencing order from version control history (first-commit timestamps per module), compute the instability index (I = Ce / (Ca + Ce), where Ce is efferent coupling and Ca is afferent coupling) for each module at the time it was first migrated using static analysis tools on the pre-migration codebase snapshot, and regress total project overrun (actual hours / estimated hours) on the mean instability index of the first 20% of modules migrated. Control for codebase size, team size, test coverage at migration start, and technology stack. Pre-register the hypothesis that mean instability index of early-migrated modules positively predicts total overrun ratio.
experimental_designs.hypothesis: A one-standard-deviation increase in the mean instability index of the first 20% of migrated modules will predict a statistically significant increase in total project overrun ratio (predicted beta: 0.3-0.5 on log scale), after controlling for all confounds.
experimental_designs.feasibility: High, given consultancy partnership. Version control history and project financial records are routinely retained. Static analysis to reconstruct coupling at a historical snapshot is technically feasible using archived build artifacts or dependency manifests. Target: 80-120 projects for adequate power. Primary challenge is standardizing overrun calculation across heterogeneous project types.

limitations: The analogy assumes legacy codebases have a stable, measurable coupling structure analogous to a wreck's physical architecture, but in practice coupling is dynamic: it changes during the migration itself as refactors alter dependency graphs, making the triage map obsolete as work proceeds, requiring periodic re-survey that has no clean salvor equivalent.

Marine salvage operates with a single recovery objective (cargo value) while legacy migration must simultaneously optimize multiple objectives including business continuity, team learning, regulatory compliance, and technical debt reduction -- the multi-objective context complicates the single-metric triage rule.

The framework prescribes abandonment as a rational strategy but organizational cultures with sunk-cost biases or 'no module left behind' mandates may make explicit abandonment politically infeasible, limiting the framework's applicability in contexts where the triage discipline cannot be enforced.

Coupling metrics (instability index, afferent/efferent coupling counts) are imperfect proxies for true migration risk: a highly coupled module with comprehensive test coverage and clear documentation may be safer to migrate early than a low-coupling module with zero tests and tribal-knowledge-only behavior specifications.

The shipwreck metaphor implies that the legacy codebase is a static artifact being recovered, but production systems continue to accrete new features and bugs during migration, analogous to a wreck that continues taking on water -- the framework does not explicitly address the risk of simultaneous legacy maintenance and migration, which is the norm rather than the exception.
potential_applications: Migration project scoping and estimation: using the coupling survey as a mandatory pre-estimation phase, with coupling-depth distribution as the primary input to a calibrated estimation model, replacing gut-feel estimates with structural metrics that have empirical predictive validity.

Technical debt prioritization for engineering roadmaps: applying the triage logic to debt reduction work in non-migration contexts, prioritizing debt reduction in low-coupling, high-dependency-count modules (which correspond to the accessible high-value cargo) and deliberately scheduling high-coupling debt work only after its dependency surface has been cleared.

Acquisition due diligence for software companies: using a rapid coupling survey of acquired codebase to estimate migration or modernization cost, with the coupling-depth distribution providing a more accurate predictor of true modernization effort than lines-of-code or age-of-codebase metrics alone.

Engineering team onboarding sequence design: new engineers joining a complex codebase should be assigned to work on low-coupling, well-tested, peripheral modules first -- the triage logic applied to knowledge acquisition -- building structural understanding from the accessible edges inward rather than beginning with the most entangled core systems.

Regulatory compliance modernization in financial services: banks and insurers maintaining COBOL or legacy mainframe systems for regulatory reporting can apply the triage framework to sequence compliance-driven modernization, identifying which reporting modules have low coupling to core ledger systems (accessible cargo) and which require deep integration work (abandon-in-place candidates), preventing regulatory deadlines from forcing deep-penetration migration that risks system-wide instability.

connections_to_other_frameworks.relationship: The Strangler Fig Pattern is the primary engineering implementation of the Salvage Priority Triage principle: it prescribes encircling legacy functionality with new code that progressively takes over traffic, which is exactly the salvor strategy of clearing surrounding accessible structure before attempting deep penetration. The triage framework provides the explicit sequencing rationale and the abandonment discipline that the Strangler Fig pattern assumes but does not articulate.

DDD Bounded Contexts define the natural isolation barriers in a complex domain, which map directly to the structural compartments the salvor uses to plan recovery sequences. A coupling survey conducted through a bounded-context lens will naturally identify intra-context coupling (which can be addressed within a single recovery operation) versus inter-context coupling (which requires multi-phase cross-boundary work), enabling more accurate triage depth estimates.

Minimum Viable Architecture prescribes identifying the smallest structural commitment that supports current requirements, deferring irreversible architectural decisions. This is compatible with the triage framework's abandonment discipline: both frameworks share the insight that attempting to resolve all structural problems simultaneously is the primary failure mode, and that controlled deferral of low-priority high-risk decisions is a feature rather than a failure.

connections_to_other_frameworks.framework: Real Options Theory (applied to software by Fichman et al.)
connections_to_other_frameworks.relationship: Real Options Theory applied to software treats architectural decisions as options -- deferring commitment until information improves -- which formalizes the economic rationale for the triage abandonment strategy. A deeply entangled legacy module is a real option to migrate later when better tooling, more complete understanding, or cleaner surrounding structure reduces the option exercise cost; exercising it prematurely destroys option value, which is the financial equivalent of the structural collapse the salvor avoids.
